graph.confusable_reason: Both are foliar fungal diseases of wheat that produce conspicuous pustule/colony masses on leaf surfaces, favor cool weather, and can be confused at a distance or early on when a grower simply sees discolored, powdery-to-fuzzy material on leaves. Color and sign morphology resolve them on close inspection, but field reports and quick scouting routinely conflate "yellow/white stuff on leaves."
graph.shared_presentation: Cool-season foliar disease of wheat presenting as discolored, raised material on leaf blades (often upper surface and lower/shaded canopy first), spreading upward through the canopy, capable of premature leaf senescence and yield loss under prolonged cool, moist conditions. Both leave powdery/dusty residue that rubs off onto fingers, and both can appear on leaf sheaths and occasionally glumes.

forks.question: What color is the fungal mass/pustule on the leaf, and does it rub off as a dust?
forks.a_signal: White to grayish-white cottony/powdery fungal colonies (mycelium + conidia) sitting ON the surface, easily wiped off leaving green tissue beneath
forks.b_signal: Bright yellow to orange (rust-colored) powdery spores erupting FROM within the leaf, staining fingers yellow-orange

forks.question: Are the signs arranged as discrete blotchy patches or as narrow linear stripes?
forks.a_signal: Irregular fluffy patches/colonies that coalesce; not confined between veins
forks.b_signal: Pustules (uredinia) line up in long narrow yellow stripes parallel to leaf veins on adult plants

forks.question: Does the fungus sit on top of the leaf or break through the epidermis?
forks.a_signal: Superficial ectoparasitic growth resting on the cuticle; underlying tissue later chlorotic
forks.b_signal: Erumpent pustules that rupture the epidermis, leaving a torn margin around each uredinium

forks.question: On which leaf surface and canopy position do signs first concentrate?
forks.a_signal: Often lower/shaded leaves and both surfaces, dense in humid lower canopy with low airflow
forks.b_signal: Primarily upper leaf surface, can move rapidly to flag leaf; stripes most diagnostic on adult flag leaves

forks.question: What temperature and moisture regime is driving the outbreak?
forks.a_signal: Cool, humid, high-density canopy WITHOUT need for free leaf wetness; thrives in moderate humidity and shade
forks.b_signal: Cool temperatures (roughly 50-60F) WITH dew/free moisture for spore germination; checked by hot weather

forks.question: How does the lesion mature late in the season?
forks.a_signal: Colonies turn gray-tan and develop tiny black cleistothecia (pinhead specks) within the mat
forks.b_signal: Late-season stripes switch to black telia (linear black stripes) producing teliospores

graph.narrative_a: Powdery mildew (Blumeria graminis f. sp. tritici) is an obligate biotroph that grows ectoparasitically, forming white-to-gray powdery colonies of mycelium and conidia that sit superficially on the leaf surface and can be wiped off to reveal green or chlorotic tissue beneath. It favors dense, lush canopies with high humidity but does not require free water, making it severe in well-fertilized, thick stands and in cool, overcast conditions. Disease starts low in the canopy and on shaded leaves, then climbs. Late in the season colonies darken and bear pinhead-sized black cleistothecia (chasmothecia) embedded in the felt-like mat, a confirming sign distinct from rust.
graph.narrative_b: Stripe rust (Puccinia striiformis f. sp. tritici) produces bright yellow-orange uredinia that erupt through the leaf epidermis and, on adult plants, line up in long narrow stripes running parallel to the veins between the leaf's vascular bundles. The orange urediniospores rub off and stain fingers yellow-orange. It is the most cold-tolerant of the wheat rusts, requiring cool temperatures (roughly 50-60F) with dew or free moisture, and it shuts down in hot weather. It can move explosively to the flag leaf, causing significant yield loss. Late in the season pustules convert to linear black telia. Stripe rust epidemics are strongly tied to cool, moist regions and seasonal weather, and inoculum often arrives via long-distance spore dispersal.
graph.management_a: Use resistant/tolerant cultivars and avoid excessive nitrogen and overly dense stands that create humid microclimates. Improve airflow through appropriate seeding rates. Scout lower canopy early. Triazole (DMI) and strobilurin (QoI) or mixed-mode fungicides applied at early disease onset (and protecting the flag leaf around heading) are effective; time applications to forecast and crop stage rather than calendar.
graph.management_b: Plant resistant varieties (key control given race shifts) and monitor regional stripe rust forecasts and spore-tracking advisories, since inoculum often blows in. Scout early and frequently in cool, wet springs, focusing on protecting the flag leaf. Apply DMI/QoI fungicides at first detection or per forecast; early-season "rust watch" timing is critical because epidemics build fast. Track virulent race emergence that can overcome deployed resistance genes.

lay.farmer_lay_report: I'm seeing dusty, discolored patches building up on the wheat leaves that rub off on my fingers, starting lower in the canopy and on the upper side of the blades, then working its way up the plant. The affected leaves are starting to fade and die back early, and it's getting worse with this stretch of cool, damp weather.
lay.decisive_lay_a: Look for white to grayish, cottony or powdery patches sitting right on top of the leaf, like a dusting of flour or talc. You can wipe it clean off with your thumb and find healthy green leaf underneath.
lay.decisive_lay_b: Look for bright yellow to orange dusty stripes running along the leaf that seem to burst out from inside the leaf, not just sit on top. When you rub them, they stain your fingers a rusty yellow-orange color.
